metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion to 0f70 requiring that ANY exception from S5 or S6 is caught and results in a graceful skip (advisory findings omitted, review proceeds normally), not just absent-LLM-config failures.
findings.finding: Child 0f70's graceful-skip only covers absence of the `[agents]` extra or API key. No child defines error handling for runtime failures of S5 (5a8f BM25F retrieval) or S6 (9022 LLM judge) during the synchronous review_plan call — arbitrary exceptions in these stages could propagate to the user and break review_plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the entire overlap step in a broad try/except in 0f70's orchestration, logging the error as a debug trace and returning zero advisory findings on any failure — consistent with the graceful-skip philosophy.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in 0f70 whether the synchronously-extracted query digest is persisted to the shared store (2d0f) or discarded, and if persisted, document the write path and its interaction with the async enrichment pipeline.
findings.finding: Child 0f70 extracts the query digest synchronously (calling the S1/ee3d op inline at review time) but does not specify whether this result is also persisted to the shared digest store (2d0f). If it is NOT persisted, the LLM extraction cost is paid on every `review_plan` invocation for the same ticket. If it IS persisted, there is an undeclared write dependency from 0f70 into 2d0f's event model that neither child acknowledges.
findings.suggested_fix: Have 0f70 check 2d0f for a fresh cached digest first, and only call ee3d if absent/stale — then optionally write the result back via 2d0f's event model. This aligns with the shared-cache intent of 2d0f.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion to e1f4 confirming that older clients preserve-and-ignore the new queue event types, mirroring 2d0f's explicit backward-compatibility AC.
findings.finding: Child e1f4 adds new queue event types to the shared event store but does NOT declare that older clients will preserve-and-ignore them — unlike 2d0f, which explicitly cites the rollout convention ('preserved-and-ignored by older clients, per the rollout convention'). Queue events are visible to all clients reading the store.
findings.location: child e1f4-d518-3240-472e — Scope and Acceptance Criteria; child 2d0f-19f2-dd15-4336 — Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Add to e1f4 AC: 'Older clients preserve-and-ignore the new queue event types (fsck WARNs on newer-than-binary event types, per the rollout convention).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a user-visible corpus coverage indicator or warning when the enriched corpus is below a meaningful threshold, so cold-store silent-degradation is distinguishable from genuine no-overlap.
findings.finding: In a cold store (no prior enrichment), stage-1 (5a8f) returns zero candidates because all corpus digests are absent/stale, so the system silently produces no overlap findings. No child defines a minimum corpus coverage threshold, a user-visible warning for the cold-start case, or a fallback — leaving parent AC1 (store-wide detection) silently unmet in practice.
findings.location: child 5a8f-d6a0-ca32-4f51 — AC3 ('Corpus tickets with absent/stale digests are skipped'); parent AC1
findings.suggested_fix: In 0f70 (or the store-health integration), emit an advisory note when the fraction of enriched tickets in the corpus is below a threshold (e.g., <50%), indicating that overlap detection coverage is limited.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the detection mechanism (e.g., embedding cosine similarity with a stated threshold, or LLM-based pairwise scoring) and the similarity cutoff that triggers a candidate flag, so an executor can act without a design decision round-trip.
findings.finding: The mechanism for detecting 'semantic overlap/duplication' is a real design choice entirely deferred to the executor — no algorithm (embedding similarity, LLM comparison, keyword matching), similarity metric, or threshold is stated. An agent cannot implement this without stopping to make a foundational architectural decision.
findings.location: Scope section / AC line 1: 'Detects semantic overlap/duplication between separate tickets store-wide'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Detection approach' subsection stating, e.g.: 'Use embedding cosine similarity (e.g., OpenAI text-embedding-ada-002 or a local model) over each ticket's title + AC text; flag pairs with similarity ≥ 0.85 as candidates. Threshold is configurable via a store-config key `overlap_threshold` (default 0.85).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the output format (e.g., structured JSON report, stdout table, or a new `validate --overlap` subcommand) and where surfaced candidates appear so the human review workflow is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The output format and integration point of the capability are unspecified — an executor cannot determine whether to produce a CLI report, a structured JSON file, a log to stdout, a new `validate` subcommand, or something else.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Closer to a store-health / scheduling capability (validate / next_batch)' and AC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reframe AC1 as a specific observable outcome (e.g., 'given a store containing tickets A and B with known semantic overlap, the tool outputs a candidate flag for that pair') and add a seed fixture or example pair to enable in-session verification.
findings.finding: AC1 ('Detects semantic overlap/duplication between separate tickets store-wide') is effort-framed ('Detects') rather than stating a specific observable outcome, and no in-session test fixture (e.g., a seeded store with known duplicate tickets) is specified to verify correctness during development.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies low false-flagging, e.g. 'Legitimately-distinct but related tickets (e.g. tickets sharing a component but performing different operations) are NOT flagged as duplicates/overlapping' or a measurable precision threshold.
findings.finding: The Success Criteria explicitly names 'Low false-flagging of legitimately-related-but-distinct work' as a quality goal, but no Acceptance Criterion verifies or measures this property. It is an uncovered success-criterion deliverable.
findings.location: Success Criteria (second bullet) — no corresponding AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] Legitimately-related-but-distinct ticket pairs (e.g. two tickets touching the same file for different purposes) are not surfaced as overlap candidates (false-positive rate remains low, validated by a representative store sample).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that semantic similarity thresholds (and any other tunable detection parameters) are configurable (e.g., via a config file or CLI flag), not hardcoded, so false-positive rates can be adjusted without code changes.
findings.finding: The plan cites 'low false-flagging' as a success criterion but specifies no configurable threshold or tuning mechanism for semantic similarity. Similarity cutoffs are inherently evolvable (e.g., cosine-distance thresholds, embedding model choice) and are prime candidates to change as the store grows, yet no configurability is called for.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Low false-flagging of legitimately-related-but-distinct work'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Similarity threshold(s) and detection parameters are exposed as configurable values (e.g., in a store config or CLI flag), with documented defaults.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or task requiring that the architectural decision (capability placement, detection approach, integration point with validate/next_batch) be captured in an ADR or CLAUDE.md update.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel store-health architectural capability (store-wide semantic analysis, integration with validate/next_batch) but calls for no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design document to record the architectural decision.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Closer to a store-health / scheduling capability (validate / next_batch)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen ACs 1 and 2 with end-state-observable proofs: specify the output format for surfaced candidates (e.g., a report listing ticket pairs with a similarity signal), and add at least one concrete test scenario (e.g., two tickets with known overlapping content must appear in the output).
findings.finding: Acceptance Criteria 1 and 2 — detecting semantic overlap and surfacing candidate relations — lack end-state-observable proofs: no example output format, no test scenario, no measurable recall/precision target, and no definition of what a 'detection' result looks like to a human reviewer.
findings.suggested_fix: AC 1 example: 'Given a store containing two tickets with substantially overlapping title/body text, running the store-health check produces a report listing that pair as a candidate duplicate.' AC 2 example: 'The report lists each candidate pair with a suggested link type (duplicates/supersedes/depends_on) and requires explicit human confirmation before any link is written.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete validation mechanism to the ACs: e.g., a known-overlap seed pair that must be detected, a false-positive rate ceiling (e.g., <20% on a labelled sample of 20 ticket pairs), or a structured dogfooding protocol where a human confirms results against a reference store snapshot.
findings.finding: No concrete validation mechanism is specified for the detection capability. 'Low false-flagging' is stated as a success criterion but is not quantified (no threshold, no baseline, no before/after workflow comparison, and no dogfooding scenario). At least one AC should carry a measurable signal (e.g., false-positive rate target, a known-duplicate pair detected in a reference store, or a manual spot-check protocol).
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Given a reference store snapshot containing N hand-labelled overlapping pairs, the detector surfaces ≥X% of them with a false-positive rate ≤Y% on a 20-pair held-out set, verified by manual spot-check before merge.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a mechanism section specifying HOW semantic overlap is detected (e.g. a lightweight pre-filter on title/tag similarity followed by LLM pairwise scoring for top candidates, or embedding-based cosine similarity over description+title), including how the approach scales to a large store and how false-positive suppression is achieved operationally.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no mechanism, algorithm, or data-flow for HOW semantic overlap/duplication is detected store-wide — the proposed mechanism is entirely absent, so its correctness cannot be verified and an implementer cannot build from this plan.
findings.location: Scope section; Acceptance Criteria (all three ACs); Success Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Consider a two-stage approach: (1) a cheap deterministic pre-filter (title token overlap, shared tags/epic, shared file_impact paths) to reduce the candidate pair set from O(n²) to a manageable subset, then (2) an LLM or embedding similarity pass over pre-filtered candidates to detect semantic overlap. State which stage lives in validate vs. next_batch, and define what output format (candidate pair + suggested link type + confidence) gets surfaced for human confirmation. This grounds AC2 (low false-flagging) in the pre-filter design rather than leaving it as an unspecified property.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concise positive rationale for the chosen detection approach once it is specified (e.g. why a pre-filter + LLM pass fits better than a pure embedding approach given rebar's store size and the need for human-confirmation outputs), so the design intent is documented alongside the mechanism.
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for a chosen approach because no approach is chosen — G6(4) requires that a plan state WHY the chosen mechanism fits, but there is no mechanism to rationalize.
findings.location: Scope section; the entire plan body

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to a concrete mechanism for semantic similarity computation (e.g. LLM-pairwise-with-batching, embedding+ANN index, BM25 retrieval, or MinHash-LSH) and justify the choice against established approaches — including scalability as the store grows and any new dependencies required.
findings.finding: The plan names 'semantic overlap/duplication detection' as a Success Criterion but never commits to any mechanism (LLM-pairwise, embedding + vector index, BM25 retrieval, MinHash-LSH, etc.). This leaves unresolved capability assertions and prevents assessment of prior-art fit, scalability, or dependency needs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Mechanism' section that: (1) names the chosen approach (e.g. 'embed each ticket title+description with the existing rebar LLM runner → store vectors in-process → ANN nearest-neighbor search to surface candidates above a cosine-similarity threshold'); (2) notes why it was chosen over simpler alternatives (BM25 over the existing `search` command, or MinHash-LSH for a zero-LLM-dependency path); (3) states the O(n) or O(n log n) complexity target to confirm store-scale feasibility.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an output schema for surfaced candidates (at minimum: ticket_a_id, ticket_b_id, suggested_relation (enum: duplicates|supersedes|depends_on), rationale, confidence_or_score) and specify the output format (JSON, CLI table, etc.) to give executors and consumers a binding contract.
findings.finding: The plan's AC states the system 'surfaces candidate duplicate/supersede/dependency relations for human confirmation' but defines no output schema: no field list, no enum for relation_type, no confidence or score field, and no format (JSON, CLI table, etc.). An executor building this capability cannot know what shape to emit.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line: 'Surfaces candidate duplicate/supersede/dependency relations for human confirmation (does not auto-mutate links).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Output schema' subsection with fields: `{ticket_a, ticket_b, suggested_relation: 'duplicates'|'supersedes'|'depends_on', rationale: str, score: float}` and state that the output is a JSON array. Reference the existing schema pattern in `src/rebar/schemas/`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a decision rule (or prompt instruction) for classifying detected pairs into the correct relation type: e.g., 'duplicates' when scope/AC are substantially identical, 'supersedes' when one ticket's scope fully replaces the other's, 'depends_on' when one ticket's implementation is a prerequisite for the other's.
findings.finding: The plan mentions that the system 'suggests `duplicates`/`supersedes`/`depends_on` links' but provides no decision rule or protocol for classifying a detected overlapping pair into one of these three distinct relation types. This is a processing protocol referenced but not specified alongside the (absent) schema.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'suggests `duplicates`/`supersedes`/`depends_on` links rather than auto-applying them'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Classification rules' note: 'duplicates — both tickets target the same functional outcome and neither adds scope beyond the other; supersedes — ticket B's scope fully subsumes or replaces ticket A's; depends_on — ticket A must be completed before ticket B can begin (structural prerequisite, not just related).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the detection mechanism (e.g., embedding cosine similarity with a named model and configurable threshold, or LLM-based pairwise scoring with a pre-filter) and any scalability strategy (e.g., pre-filter by ticket type/status before pairwise comparison) so an executor can implement without an architectural decision round-trip.
findings.finding: The plan names a 'semantic overlap/duplication' detection mechanism as the core of the capability but specifies no algorithm, model, or agent protocol — leaving the entire detection approach undefined for an executor. This is an undefined processing protocol for the central sub-step.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 1: 'Detects semantic overlap/duplication between separate tickets store-wide (cross-epic AND non-epic), beyond deterministic file-impact and manual links.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a `validate`/`next_batch` integration path for store-wide overlap detection, and remove or restructure 0f70's wiring so the overlap step does NOT run inside `review_plan`/the `5fd2` gate.
findings.finding: Child 0f70 wires the overlap step INTO `review_plan` (a per-ticket path), directly contradicting parent AC3 which states this capability must NOT run inside the `5fd2` gate and must be a store-health capability closer to `validate`/`next_batch`. No sibling implements a `validate` or `next_batch` integration, leaving AC3 entirely uncovered.
findings.location: child 0f70-6d61-2b78-4c34 — 'What' section and AC1; parent AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sibling (or extend an existing one) to integrate store-wide overlap detection into `rebar validate` or `next_batch` as a store-health sweep. 0f70 may optionally expose a lightweight advisory surface in review_plan but must not be the primary execution path; alternatively, 0f70 should be reframed so its invocation is from a store-health trigger, not per-ticket review.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
